desc:
The plains to the west and south are slowly turning into a forest. Sparse trees
shade the plains, and a dark line of a forest is visible to the north-east. The
grass under the trees is green and healthy, protected from harsh sunlight which
dries the vegetation in open plains.
name: Forested Plains

desc:
A trampled path surrounded by high grass and a few bushes follows the course of
a small brook. The muddy brown water flows slowly, covered by a layer of green
and yellow algae prospering in it. In the east the path turns away from the
brook and heads towards the line of forest visible near the horizon.
name: Turn in a Trail

desc:
A giant fir tree towers above the forest, its magnificent crown soaring high
above in the sky. Its trunk is thick and knobby, indicating the tree's age, yet
the upper branches look young and fresh. Strong branches at regular intervals
form a natural spiral staircase around the trunk, and looking up, a wooden
platform is visible resting on upper branches.
name: Near an Old Outlook

desc:
The woodland area is sparsely populated with young firs and tall pines, which
have filled the forest floor with a soft carpet of thorns. The forest gets
denser to the east but opens up into plains to the west, where the trees
suddenly end. To the north and south the light forest continues, however in the
north the conifers are replaced with alders and other hardwood.
name: Light Forest

desc:
A few scattered alders with dark grey barks and white beeches with black
markings grow on a slope gently rising eastwards. The trees are sparse, with
wild grasses and ferns growing around them. In the north, where the ground gets
rockier, the trees disappear completely, giving way to low yellowish grass. To
the west, however, the slope suddenly ends with a cliff too deep to descend.
name: Scattered Trees on a Slope

desc:
The rocky plateau in the north and east suddenly falls off at a cliff in the
west. The sheer face of rock drops vertically from the ledge, offering almost no
support for climbing down. Forty feet below a grassy slope spreads out to the
horizon, broken by the silhouettes of a few pines. Heading northwards a small
trail seeks path among the massive boulders lying on the ground.
name: A Cliff above the Fields

desc:
The rocky field is covered by innumerable small rocks and few larger boulders.
The stones are fringed with dense profusions of heather and gorse, which are the
only plants in the area besides some stunted mountain pines. To the west, the
ground slopes slightly downwards, where heather is replaced with clumps of
brownish grass growing amid the scattered rubble. To the north a dark line of a
trail running across the plateau is visible.
name: Rocky Field beside a Trail

desc:
Tall grasses grow densely together with large bushes. Sharp thorns, briars and
brambles all strive for space and entwine together forming a thick mesh of
foliage. The grass seems to be fighting a losing battle against the large bushes
that dominate the immediate surroundings.
name: Bushy Plain

desc:
A long desolation has taken its toll on what looks like the abandoned courtyard
of once a strong farm. Mixed shrubs and trees have grown around the old well,
concealing in the shade the chaos of the overturned tiles. A ruined arch stands
to the south, possible remnant of a taller construction around a more imposing
entrance. Its pillars are still sturdy enough to support the current crude gate.
name: An Overgrown Courtyard

desc:
Most of the masonry of this old building has crumbled away after centuries of
wilderness assaults and is now scattered on the ground under a significant layer
of organic decay. The northern wall and a pillar to the southeast still stand,
while the rest has been more recently replaced by rough planks or bound dead
branches.
name: Remnants of a Pen

desc:
With the northern wall crumbled away and the eastern one collapsed over a larger
pile of rubble outside, this part of the farm does not offer much shelter.
Furthermore, the remaining sections are covered by a thick layer of black soot,
turned into a dark crust by the passing of time. However, fallen trees have been
laid on the northern foundations and lean on the top of the southern wall, their
dead foliage forming a makeshift roof. Debris of masonry, decayed wood and soil
partly cover the old floor tiles.
name: Blackened Walls

desc:
Not nearly as ruined as the rest of the building, this room actually has a roof
albeit in the form of the wide foliage of a tree. This oak has been spreading
its roots under the floor, slowly reclaiming the place to nature. The rest of
the structure is still upright, with a small niche in the southern wall and two
stone blocks carved as benches and embedded in the foundations near the western
window.
name: A Ruin under a Wide Tree

desc:
The construction that once stood here is no more. The base of the walls may
still exist, but as everything else has collapsed on top of them they are
unlikely to be seen ever again from here. The resulting high heap of broken
masonry is treacherous to walk over but provides a great view to the landscape
around.
name: Atop a Pile of Rubble

desc:
Rays of sunlight pass through cracks in the walls during daytime, and a puddle
of water formed on the earthen floor at the far end corner suggests that the
roof is leaking water. Some rusty tools are hanging on a wall over a small bed,
and another, larger bed is placed next to a tiny window. Blackened bits of wood
lie scattered under a rusty iron pot suspended by a tripod. A trail passing
through the plains can be seen from the window.
name: An Abandoned Hut

desc:
Inside the building, broken objects scatter the floor; old furniture, broken
jars and something that looks like the remains of a large animal. There is a
faint, acrid smell in the air and some smoke can be seen slowly drifting from
the cracks in the floor. Whole floor is covered by dust and litter, except a
rectangular area near the door.
name: Ruins of a Building

desc:
Down here the air is cold and damp, and green mould and lichen are growing on
the moist stone walls of the cellar. Parts of the walls are stained with a rusty
red tinge, which looks like blood. One pair of shackles is hanging on the wall,
the debris strewn on the floor around them indicate that they have been used
recently.
name: A Damp Cellar

desc:
Several crude pallets line the walls here, with rough stained blankets strewn on
top. A pail in the corner with a large wet stain around it suggests that it is
being used as a makeshift waste bucket. This room is also quite damp, with small
rivulets of water trickling down the walls.
name: Sleeping Quarters

desc:
Small flowers grow amidst lush, soft grasses here. Blue, pink and yellow spots
break up the otherwise green pasture. Considering its short, almost well-tended
appearance, it seems that this pasture is a popular grazing area for the local
wildlife. The large meadow is surrounded by the tall grasses of the plain.
name: Secluded Meadow

desc:
The grass grows very tall and thick here, surrounding everything in an almost
claustrophobic way. The landscape is filled by green-shaded grass moving in a
slow fashion. To the west stands a hill, its peak poking through the grasses,
and to the north the grass transforms into dense shrubbery; bushes, vines and
branches tangling together forming a dense wall of mixed vegetation.
name: Dense Plain

desc:
The plain here is crossed by a faint line that once could have been a path. It
comes from the west and leads east towards Mirkwood. No signs of recent usage
are visible, and it seems that the creatures that tracked this path are not
using it anymore.
name: Vanishing Path in a Plain

desc:
The grass on these verdant plains grows thinner here, allowing various types of
plants and flowers to populate this area. There is a large gorge to the west,
while to the south the smoothly flowing plains transform into an entangled mess
of bushes, grass, and other vegetation.
name: Thin Plain

desc:
A small wooden platform rests in the upper branches of a huge fir tree, with a
hole in the middle to ease descent down. Remains of a handrail still surround
the platform, but all of the ropes are gone and some of the posts decayed,
giving little or almost no support to prevent falling down. High crowns of trees
in the east block any view to the forest, but in the west a dry riverbed is
clearly visible during daytime.
name: Old Outlook

desc:
Massive, tangled roots cascade down the round shaft forming a natural stairway
and allowing easy passage to both directions. Essential to the thriving life on
the surface, water trickles down a fissure and forms a clear pool between the
jagged rocks of the bottom. Half-way up, a small passage opens to the east but
is abruptly cut off by heavy rocks.
name: Down a Well Shaft

desc:
The trail leads east and west, getting wider on the rocky plateau to the east.
The ground here slopes gradually down, as it approaches the grasslands to the
west. Rocks and boulders of different sizes are scattered around the area,
hindering movement off the trail. Sparse yellowish grass and low bushes cover
the ground, providing hiding spots for lizards and smaller animals. A few feet
south of the trail stands an old, blackened tree, struck by a lightning.
name: A Wide Trail on Rocky Ground

desc:
A small hill suddenly rises from the surrounding plains which go on for miles in
every direction. From atop of the hill the dark line of a trail running across a
plateau is visible to the south. In contrast to the tough grasses around, the
hill is overgrown with juicy, high grass, interspersed with clusters of wild
flowers, which roughly outline the foot of the hill.
name: A Small Hill

desc:
A field of coarse yellow weeds extends towards the northern horizon of this dry
plain. The grass-covered earth is made up of a grainy mixture of sand and soil
which holds tufts of long grasses in large patches along with the smaller
varieties. The ground becomes rockier to the south and a pebbled trail can be
seen heading towards the east into some long rolling valleys.
name: Sandy Field

desc:
Thin shards of limestone layer the ground at the foot of this falling ridge. A
large number of grey slabs litter the ground as they slowly erode into the
grassy plains that sprawl to the north and south. A deep valley can be seen to
the south that extends to a forest. The stone-lined trail leads to the east and
west wandering through the rocky plains alongside a valley-dip to the south.
name: Rocky Trail

desc:
A gritty, sandy plain sprawls across this lonesome expanse. Some withered tufts
of long grass grow among the smaller heather in the rocky ground. To the south,
a trail can be seen on a rising ridge of yellow and green grass. The ridge heads
eastwards and then twists around towards the north, where it falls.
name: Sandy Plain

desc:
Soft, grey pebbles litter the ground among the gritty sand particles that make
up the dark earth. Long green and yellow grasses extend across the plains in
soft rolling heaps around deposits of heather and rock. A rising ridge can be
seen extending and falling towards the east from its southern start.
name: Sandy Plain

desc:
This sandy trail turns at the feet of two ridges. The smaller ridge to the west
is dotted with rocks, while the larger grass-covered ridge sprawls eastwards. To
the south extends a grassy plain of heather leading up to a dark forest in the
far distance. Several woody bushes grow on the embankment, forming a mound.
name: Turn in the Sandy Trail

desc:
This sandy field extends along the base of a long, rising ridge that continues
eastwards. Small plants and grasses grow in the gritty, dark earth in yellow and
green tufts around the grey rocks that litter the area. Some woody shrubs can be
seen in the east where the ridge starts to fall again towards the north.
name: Sandy Field

desc:
Hidden away below the highest point of the ridge and its two falling slopes lies
this field of brambles and berries. The shrubs' thick roots dig into the sandy
earth among the exposed limestone bedrock at the bottom of the ridge. Animal
droppings cover the ground amidst the heather and grass where the brambles have
been picked clean of their berries by the local wildlife.
name: Shrubland Field

desc:
The easterly limestone ridge falls into the expanse of the yellow plains. The
grey rocks crumble at the edge of the ridge, where woody shrubs of berry-laden
brambles grow thick in the sandy earth. Long grasses poke out from around the
slabs of the element-worn rocks. A quilt of multicoloured lichen smothers a
misshapen boulder that silently stands watch among the brambles.
name: Rocky Slope

desc:
A lonely plain of withered grasses and slabs of stone sits on the leveled ridge.
The ridge continues sloping downwards to the west, but rises in the east where
it then curves southwards. Small piles of rabbit droppings can be seen in
between the thickets of grasses and short, woody shrubs that grow here.
name: Sullen Plains

desc:
The ridge continues its downward climb towards the north, until it reaches a
great chasm. The brown grasses that wither on the ground become sparse as they
reach the cracked edge. A lonely, splintered tree grows on the northern side of
the desolate ridge, hanging precariously over a cliff's edge, where its roots
desperately cling to the dirt in between slabs of coarse, sandy stone.
name: Ridge by a Splintered Tree

desc:
The ridge branches into two falling valleys to the east and north from its
westerly shrub-covered height. The northern, rocky slope is covered in a brown
layer of withered grass, while a dried creek bed follows the eastern slope down
into a wide and lush valley of tiny, woody shrubs and green grasses.
name: Falling Ridge

desc:
This falling ridge-top is surrounded by woody shrubs and small grasses. In the
centre of the ridge is an old campfire ring encircled by a couple comfortable
stones. The area looks fairly disused, judging from the encompassing view from
which valleys extend in every direction. Below the ridge to the south, an old
trail can be seen along with a dark forest even farther in the distance.
name: Makeshift Campsite

desc:
A dried creek bed filled with smooth stones continues down the side of the
western ridge over the limestone bedrock into a wide valley of shrubs and vivid
green grasses. The creek bed then turns its way southward as it cuts through the
wildflower-covered steppe as the surrounding ridges continue falling to the
north. The valley is filled with lush, rolling grasses and woody shrubs.
name: Foot of a Ridge

desc:
The grassy ridge bends to the north into a sward as the trail follows the slopes
downwards to the east and west. Along the trail embankment, a green thicket sits
among exposed, limestone bedrock. To the south, and beyond the ridge's edge,
extend rolling valleys that stretch on far into the distance, where a dark
forest can be spotted, along with a larger one far to the east.
name: Crest in a Ridge Trail

desc:
The overgrown, rocky trail leads over the easterly rising ridge, among the grey
slabs of limestone that jut out of the ground, from its western source. Several
large shrubs grow around the slabs in patches, providing some cover for animals
which have left their droppings in small piles. A grassy valley spreads out to
the south before reaching a dark wood, topped with pines, in the far distance.
name: Trail on a Ridge

desc:
The rising ridge fans out to the south into a wide steppe of coarse yellow
grass. Tufts of long grasses cover the plains in patches among the shorter
heather and the occasional rock that pokes out from the expanse. Even farther to
the south sits a dark forest. The trail leads up the ridge to the east from its
western source. A small patch of woody shrubs grows along the trail.
name: Sandy Trail

desc:
The trail sits at the bottom of two ridges where a small grassy valley sprawls
to the north and south. The trail bends here, from the north and then to the
east, up the rising ridge of yellow grass and heather. The land to the south
looks much greener than that to the sandy north. Even further to the south, a
dark forest topped with great pines can be seen past the rolling valleys.
name: Bend in the Sandy Trail

desc:
The flat steppe extends across the east until it reaches a rocky crag that
plunges down into a wide valley of yellow and green grasses. Some woody shrubs
grow on the plains in dense patches among the rocks, along with some delicate
wild flowers. A distant trail can be seen to the north leading westward.
name: Field of Heather

desc:
A steppe of coarse grass extends here towards the south, where it slowly slopes
downwards into a small valley before a darkened wood. Several wild flowers of
white and blue grow in the rich soil that covers the plains. Small thickets dot
the steppe in dense patches. A trail can be seen to the north leading away.
name: Field of Coarse Grass

desc:
The long ridge ends here from its northeastern source in a field of yellow and
green heather. To the east, the ridge falls into another wide, craggy valley of
rolling plains. Among the rocks to the south, some small ferns grow around a
couple of lone pine trees. Several miles to the south, rises a dark forest.
name: Field of Heather

desc:
A lonesome expanse of rocky plains extends to the northwest and dominates the
horizon. The grey, limestone bedrock that exposes itself among the shrubs that
blanket the field is weather-worn and seems to continue onward to the north. The
ground slowly buckles downwards into a sinkhole to the south. Further to the
southeast is a dark forest peaked with birch and oak.
name: Coarse Shrubland

desc:
Large boulders are strewn across the yellow plains, half-buried in the thick
grass and heather that carpets the area with its coarse blades. The rocky
terrain continues on westwards, filling the horizon along with the grassy ridges
in the north and east, as a dark forest stands to the south.
name: Stony Terrain

desc:
Thick slabs of limestone lie in a pile of rubble to the north where the ridge
has heavily eroded. Throughout the rocky mess grow a large number of bushy
shrubs with roots that dearly hold on to the sparse soil. The number of bushes
decreases as the land becomes more rocky towards the south.
name: Shrubland Field

desc:
A field of stumps sits against the backdrop of a dark wood, while a dry plain
stretches on towards the valley-filled horizon to the north. The fir stumps ooze
a pungent yellow sap down their boles in coagulated clusters. The coarse heather
has been trampled into mud riddled with lines from all the timber which has been
dragged through the track into a large pile of wood to the east.
name: Clearcut Field

desc:
Brown and orange leaves fill the crevices in the limestone where the ground has
buckled into a sinkhole, taking the forest down with it. Dirty white strips of
birch bark litter the ground around the trees where their boles reach up into
the sky to be topped with delicate leaves. Small ferns and grasses cover the
ground in a display of colour around a few jutting slabs of limestone.
name: Sunken Birch Glade

desc:
This forest of alder thickens considerably in the southeast as firs and oaks
replace the birches. Underneath the sparse canopy, a field of small grasses and
moss grow in between the niches where the limestone bedrock reaches the soil.
The land slowly dips down into a sinkhole to the north, revealing some plains.
name: Scattered Forest

desc:
The densely-packed fir trees extend high up into the sky and fill the air with a
tangy smell of pine. Their straight trunks extend out to brown branches covered
with prickly, green needles. In the moist earth below, a bed of fir needles
blanket the dark brown roots amid the soft grasses and ferns.
name: Dark Fir Grove

desc:
The trail follows along the foot of a dark forest that stretches towards the
east and northwest. From the forest canopy extend a number of dark firs, which
fill the air with the scent of resin. To the south extends a prairie that dips
into a shallow valley in the distance, where a patch of shrubs dot the slope.
name: Trail by a Forest

desc:
A small trail, mostly overgrown with grass, meanders through the plains.
Vegetation surrounding the trail is dense with green patches of rushes and
blackberry. Even though the trail is the only easy passage through the plains,
it shows little signs of use except a few, rare traces of animal life. The trail
makes a smooth turn from the south to the east, continuing in both directions.
To the west the plains seem to continue for miles whilst sparse trees to the
north-east indicate a change in the vegetation.
name: Bend in a Trail

desc:
A lowland valley spreads across the ground in a shallow dip as the land falls
from the northern forest and then rises again towards the south. A small, woody
patch of dark bushes grows on the southern slope among several limestone slabs.
Amidst the grasses that carpet the plains, a number of mole mounds can be seen.
name: Shallow Valley

desc:
Except for on the far east, where the vegetation becomes clearly lighter, the
grass grows high in all directions. Traces of animals passing by now and then
lead through the grass straws. The absence of thick undergrowth makes movement
here quite easy.
name: Green Meadow

desc:
Among the high grass, a couple of firs reach tall with needles healthily
pointing up towards the sky. The fresh water from the small pond has let them
grow high, creating a landmark that stands out in the flat landscape.
name: Stand of Firs

desc:
Shaded by bushes and a couple of smaller trees, this grassy plain is a place
where many animals come to rest. Droppings and a jumble of animal prints tell
the story of a active wildlife. To the southeast the grass quickly becomes
lower.
name: Tranquil Copse

desc:
The animals populating these fields keep the grass short. Compared to the tall
grass to the west, the vegetation here is significantly lower and gradually
becomes even lower to the east. The visibility over the flat landscape is good
in all directions.
name: Low Grass

desc:
Bushes of various sizes and kinds are spread out in the field, some with leaves
and others with needles. They offer room where small animals can hide from
predators that might pass by.
name: Field of Bushes

desc:
A wide field stretches far in all directions, presenting a great number of
grasses. Complemented with a stand of firs to hide from the sun under and a pond
to drink from to the north, this area would be the perfect place for cows and
other animals to go grazing.
name: Meadow of Grasses

desc:
The field is covered with a huge amount of flowers of different kinds. The
shifting colours almost make a full spectrum, looking like a rainbow. To the
south, the ground slopes downwards into a dip in the plains.
name: Field of Flowers

desc:
Rows of trees and bushes have split the low growing grass into four smaller
oblong fields. Each of the fields has a slightly different kind of grass, with
the rightmost field's grass growing really low and the grass of the other fields
growing gradually higher.
name: Four Fields

desc:
At the verge of the southern plateau, a magnificent view of the grassy lowlands
lying many miles to south can be seen. To the west, something that looks an
awful lot like a border promptly marks the beginning of a field with low grass,
while the plain to the east has no vegetation at all.
name: Grassy Plateau

desc:
It is hard to tell whether it was the cattle of the farm to the north that once
cleared this field of grass or if it was the deed of some evil force. Either
way, nowadays nothing grows here and the shapes of the ruin to the north is the
only thing that stands out on the horizon.
name: Wasteland

desc:
This ruin of a house is all that is left of a once thriving farm. The great
fence posts that made up the encircling structure of the large cow pasture are
still standing, but have broken apart and are not connected to one another
anymore. There is evidence of a fire in the way of some blackened, splintered
pieces of the fence that are still spread on the hard ground.
name: At the Ruin of a Farm

desc:
Low brush covers the ground and constitutes a change in the otherwise grassy
fields. Southwards, the brush grows higher and thicker, preventing movement in
that direction. The bushes to the west are not very thick, allowing some travel
without too many difficulties. A ruin of a farm can be seen far to the east.
name: Low Brush

desc:
As the grass was probably grazed away by cattle the soil has been blown away by
erosion making the sandy, hard ground not hospitable enough for more foliage to
grow back. Instead, thorny brush now grows here in a twisted manner.
name: Thorny Brushlands

desc:
This place has a certain air of death because of all the carcasses and skeletons
of various farmland animals lying around in various degrees of decay. None of
the skeletons have any flesh left on the bones, but are spread around on the
ground among some dark stains.
name: At a Pile of Bones

desc:
Except for a few dark grasses, reaching only inches from the ground, nothing
grows here. These dark, grey sandy plains stretch in all directions, looking an
awful lot much like a plain of charcoal and ash. The sandy plains end on the
brink of a deep fault.
name: Sandy Plains

desc:
Crags break through the grassy surface all over the plains, forcing any
traveller to be careful not to stumble. The ruin of an old farm can be seen to
the south. Cattle from that farm may have once grazed on this field.
name: Craggy Field

desc:
Whatever once grew here is long gone. The hard soil lies uncovered, save for
some usual stones of various sizes spread around. The ground is so dry that
there are cracks on the ground, and occasionally a gust of wind spreads dust in
the air. There are no signs of any animal life and the barren landscape seems to
continue to the southeast.
name: Dead Ground

desc:
The moorlands stretch far to the north and east, while some trees and bushes
present some variety in the landscape to the west. The green grass gradually
turns darker to the south, ending in an impassable wall of what seems to be
thorny, dead brush.
name: Low Grass

desc:
The wide-stretched moorlands end before halting at a deep fault to the east. The
grass has been trampled near the fault where a steep, rough and wide slide leads
downwards to it. Some traces of the animals that have been hunted down, off the
end of the brink of the fault, can be seen below. The plains continue to the
north and west but travelling south is hindered by a ridge.
name: Plain Nearing a Fault

desc:
The falling ridge makes a sudden, sheer drop as it meanders onward to the
southeast among the limestone slabs that stick out of its surface. A fine cover
of lichen and moss smothers the bedrock that has been exposed. The trail
continues onward to the west and south, within its visible embankment.
name: Turn in a Ridge Trail

desc:
Small, yellow grasses poke out from among the rocks and pebbles that cover this
ridge as it falls from the north towards a valley in the east. Further to the
south, a steep slope prevents anyone from straying from the path. A large valley
can be seen to the southwest that slowly reaches a dark forest in the distance.
The bedrock exposes itself across the ridge, dividing the vegetation.
name: Bend in a Ridge Trail

desc:
A green valley filled with rolling grasses lies enclosed between two yellow
ridges, forming a wide plain. Small bushes grow in thickets on the slopes, in
between slabs of limestone. The larger ridge can be seen climbing upwards to the
southeast while the heather-covered valley continues towards the north parted by
a pebble-covered trail that climbs up the slopes of both ridges.
name: Trail through the Valley Dip

desc:
A green field of lush heather grows across the wide valley that sits in between
two large ridges that fall towards the north. A dried creek bed continues
through the middle of the ridge from the north, where it then sinks into the
wide expanse of prairie and disappears. Several limestone boulders line the
western side of the valley, covered in lichen and surrounded by woody shrubs.
name: Pebbled Valley of Heather

desc:
The shrub-covered ridge falls towards the west as it strikes an enormous
limestone precipice to the north. Beyond the rubble that lies at its base, and
the grasses that weave in between the stones, a trail courses towards the south
and east as it follows the ridge. A grassy valley can be seen to the west.
name: Turn in a Ridge Trail

desc:
The grassy trail forks directly below the crest of the ridge, where each path
then follows a falling steppe to the north, south, and east. The old, pebbled
trails, lined by heather and surrounded by shrubs, weave against the backdrop of
an enormous, dark forest to the east and a smaller wood to the south. Before
each forest, craggy ridges and valleys extend across the rolling landscape.
name: Fork in a Ridge Trail

desc:
The trail dips down slightly as the ridge falls between two crests in the west
and east, revealing a glimpse of a dark and rocky coombe to the south. A thick
and overgrown embankment of woody shrubs grows along the crag to the north,
opposite a grassy valley that stretches out to the south. Similarly, a wall of
shrubs grows to the west among the patches of heather, forming a dense sward.
name: Trail on a Ridge

desc:
The ridge crests here before falling slightly towards the west. Beyond the
grassy trail embankment to the north, a filthy mire covered with mist sits in
the far distance to the northwest, while an enormous, dark forest grows in the
east, taking up the entire horizon. The trail weaves through a patch of dense
woody shrubs as it forks between the western, southern, and eastern slopes.
name: Turn in a Ridge Trail

desc:
Beyond the grassy trail embankment, two dark forest can be spotted in the
distance. One sits in the west and another in the far east. The eastern wood,
despite being further away, looks far darker and more menacing, as if a shadow
keeps a grasp upon it. Several limestone boulders line the trail as the ridge
extends further to the north, and then starts falling towards the west.
name: Trail on a Ridge

desc:
The grassy ridge starts rising above the land, turning into a wide steppe as it
travels towards the north. The trail weaves northward and southward along the
ridge, where it dips into a switchback towards the valley below. The limestone
bedrock is exposed to the air, where lichen and moss grow upon its surface.
name: Trail on a Ridge

desc:
The shrub-covered ridge rises towards the north from its southwestern foot as an
overgrown trail leads through it. From beyond the trail embankment, a yellow and
green steppe of heather stretches over the ridge as it extends itself until
reaching the rocky crags, where it falls into the valleys along its sides.
name: Bend in a Ridge Trail

desc:
The grassy ridge leads eastwards and westwards alongside a steep, grass-lined
crag, which nestles a valley in between another ridge that can be seen several
miles to the north. Beyond the pebbled trail's southern embankment, the land
extends into an abrupt steppe that falls down a natural, stone stair into the
dense canopy of a dark forest topped with alders and oaks.
name: Trail on a Ridge

desc:
The trail wanders along a raised steppe that stretches northwards until reaching
a sheer slope covered with woody shrubs. To the southwest, the dark forest can
be seen continuing alongside the trail. The western plains are filled with
craggy valleys of grass and stone that roll into the distant horizon. To the
west, a white formation of rocks stands near the forest.
name: Turn in a Ridge Trail

desc:
The heather that has grown over the trail becomes significantly denser as it
reaches a small bubbling creek. The tiny creek, easily hopped across, trickles
out of the southern ridge and curves alongside the trail on its northern course
until it parts ways and disappears into the dark forest. The overgrown trail
heads westwards along a falling forest-covered ridge into some white rocks.
name: Twisting Trail along a Creek

desc:
The sun-bleached remains of a couple of walls stand here among the grasses and
weeds that have retaken an ancient building. A worn trail navigates around the
building, leading east and west. The rubble of the ruins continues into the dark
forest to the south, only to be disrupted by a cavern leading downwards. To the
north, a craggy steppe extends into a valley.
name: Trail around Some Ancient Ruins

desc:
The white remains of an ancient, fallen tower rest in its coffin of wet roots
and mosses of this dark wood. The trunks of the twisted trees reach high up into
the impenetrable canopy, locking in everything. The scarred remains of the tower
lie in shattered disarray to the east and south, blocking all passage.
name: Ancient Ruins in a Dark Forest

desc:
The vast prairie expands across a rolling steppe in a show of yellow and green
among the valleys extending northwards. A dark forest stands in the distance to
the south, peaked with firs and oaks. A large, overgrown trail can be seen
twisting towards the northeast before it disappears behind a grass-covered slope
of woody shrubs, where the bedrock breaks through the surface.
name: Vast Prairies

desc:
The old trail meanders from east to west along the dark border of an ancient
forest of fir and birch. A number of deep scars have been carved through the
trail from the north towards the east, leaving fresh timber debris littering the
ground, where ferns and grasses have taken up root. Beyond the track a wide,
rolling prairie extends in a cascade of green and yellow colour.
name: Trail by a Forest

desc:
The trail wanders into the southern forest and continues eastwards along the
forest border. An unending view of the northern valleys meets the eye beyond the
trail embankment that drops steeply to the north. Weeds and ferns litter the
trail due to disuse, growing wildly among a thick tangle of roots that crawls
out of the shade from looming forest's dark canopy.
name: Twisting Trail by a Forest

desc:
Under the cover of the thick forest canopy, the trail twists between the roots
and fallen branches that litter the area. The path is completely overgrown with
green grasses and prickly shrubs that grow where the canopy breaks and the sky
breaches through. The fallen remains of a knotted oak lies along the trail in
the shade of the forest, now completely covered with grasses and fungi.
name: Twisting Trail in a Forest

desc:
The old trail divides two huge glades of trees as it stretches eastwards through
the forest. The ground to the west is covered with an orange blanket of fir
needles, while the north and east are mostly covered with a rotting patchwork of
multicoloured oak leaves. The sky barely breaks through the forest canopy.
name: Twisting Trail in a Forest

desc:
The twisting trail leads into the northern dark wood, surrounded by gnarled oaks
and straight firs towering high up into the sky. The overgrown trail continues
westerly along the forest, surrounded by a raised embankment covered in ferns,
moss, and yellow grasses. A rolling plain can be seen to the south, dipping into
valleys, which are covered with small bushes and shrubs.
name: Twisting Trail by a Forest

desc:
An enormous, charred oak provides shade among the smaller trees. A grievous scar
splits the tree where lightning struck it. Charcoal and soot cover the forest
floor where burned trunks make their way up to a rich canopy of leaves. The
underbrush below the resilient, green trees have since grown anew.
name: Charred Dark Forest

desc:
The valley floor is covered in a lush meadow of green and yellow heather. The
rich soil is topped with a fine carpet of velvet moss proliferating in between
the blades of grass. Some small, woody bushes grow on a southern rising slope,
where some young saplings have taken root among some large, stone slabs.
name: Lush Meadow

desc:
A grassy prairie expands to the south, overshadowed by the dark forest topped
with high oaks in the north. A dense wall of underbrush grows among the roots to
the north, preventing entrance into the forest. The ground is covered in a
carpet of yellow heather and fern fronds as the plains continue southward.
name: Moorland by a Forest

desc:
Sitting at the roots of an ominous forest of dark pine, the moors expand into a
wide grassland reaching into the southern valleys on the horizon. The soil that
makes up the moors is covered by heather and a carpet of moss, packed with
small, grey pebbles that dot the ground. The dark forest to the east is
enveloped by a dense tangle of snaking vines that form an impassable wall.
name: Grassland by a Forest

desc:
A glade of brown oaks grows in the northern wood filled with spade-like orange
and green leaves. The forest floor teams with ferns and delicate grasses which
flourish among the pebbles and moss. A darker stand of firs grows in the eastern
forest, softly carpeting the ground in an orange blanket of needles.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
Among the gnarled roots, rotting leaves, and patches of grass that cover the
ground, sit several smooth shards of white rock. Their unnatural shape hint of
their man-made origin, but if a structure once stood here, these rocks are all
that remain. Up above, twisted branches interlock under the gaze of a towering,
dark oak in the middle of the woods, preventing nearly all light from entering.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
This is probably the highest spot of the sides that surround the fault below. It
is obvious why the hunters didn't choose the precipice directly to the north for
hunting animals down into the fault; there would not be anything left of any
animal, or man for that matter, to eat if it has taken the fall to the north.
The view of the area below is unlimited and straight across the fault to the
north, lies a forest.
name: Precipice above a Fault

desc:
The muddy track turns southwards here from over the declining, grassy slope to
the west. Beyond the slope, a dark forest sits in the distance, while to the
north a green plain of heather and stone rolls across the horizon. The northern
embankment thins slightly where an animal trail breaks through the underbrush.
name: Track in a Moors

desc:
A muddy track from the west leads eastward in a grassy field. Small broken twigs
and wooden shards litter the thick mud in long scar-like grooves. Some pungent,
axe-hewn fir branches have been laid on the track in a haphazard pile,
preventing any further progress westward. The ground sharply rises up to the
south, where the tips of a distant, dark forest peak out from beyond the slope.
name: Track in a Moors

desc:
The rolling valley dips slightly as it continues northwards, spreading into a
vivid prairie of green and yellow heather. Several crags scour the land as the
limestone bedrock exposes itself to the thick swards of grasses. A distant
ridge-wall can be seen climbing in the north, opposite the forest to the south.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
A wide prairie extends towards the east from a craggy ridge-wall in a display of
green and yellow colour among the many grey, limestone slabs that have fallen
from the cliff. The land to the south slopes up slightly to a darkened forest of
which only the tops of fir trees can be seen.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
A shrub-covered crag ends its northern course and heads eastwards along the
valley floor, as the grassy prairie extends in all other directions. A show of
yellow and green sweeps across the land from all the thick grasses that grow
among the grey slabs of bedrock. To the north, a ridge can be seen that carries
itself from the east to a high crest where it then falls to the west.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
Yellow and green grasses grow in between two shrub-dotted crags as this valley
sprawls to the east and west. The rocky crags that line the valley scale upwards
to an unclimbable ridge, where the grassy southern ridge climbs and the rocky
northern ridge falls toward the east. A sole fir tree stands to the south,
surrounded by a dense thicket of woody shrubs among fallen needles.
name: Field of Heather

desc:
An expansive stretch of small bushes grows in between the two ridge-walls that
enclose this valley. Long grasses of green and yellow grow in the field, among
intricate wild flowers and ferns. Along the rocky edge of the valley sit a
number of lichen-speckled boulders that sink into the vivid grasses, which
spread into the wide, western prairie that continues on towards the horizon.
name: Shrubland Valley

desc:
Weeds and shrubs line the craggy face of the ridge that leads upwards at a sheer
angle. The limestone bedrock has exposed itself, revealing delicate, stacked
layers of grey and brown. Grasses and mosses pockmark the stones as they slowly
erode into a fine dust that will sink into the southern plains.
name: Yellow Meadow

desc:
This yellow meadow sits at the bottom of a steep ridge. Rubble from the ridge's
craggy face has fallen and formed a half-buried pile. Since collapsing, the
rubble from the ridge has been reclaimed by a grassland as it slowly extends
itself towards the south. Clusters of large, prickly weeds grow among the grey,
lichen-covered rocks where soil has collected.
name: Yellow Meadow

desc:
Grey fungal spores float through the dry air of the alder and birch wood, which
is hidden deep within a rocky crag. A foul, decaying blanket of leaves covers
the ground where mould and mushrooms poke out from in between the white strips
of birch bark and rotting branches. With each rotten branch that falls from the
canopy above, a furious explosion of sickening spores become airborne.
name: Rotting Dark Wood

desc:
The canopy breaks in several places, revealing the sky through the branches and
leaves. A large number of mushrooms grow near the northern birch and fir trees,
in patches where the fern fronds and grasses have thinned. A light mist creeps
in from the west, followed by the smell of fir. A stand of gnarled, dark oaks
rustle in the southwest, their twisted branches rising upwards like claws.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
The limestone bedrock breaks the surface of the roots, fallen leaves, and soft
moss where two ridges steeply meet. Small trickles of water dribble down the
rocky slopes among the roots and grasses into the ground. A small, unreachable
cave can be seen above a large, mossy slab in the steep wall, while mist clings
to the ground underneath the dark canopy that rises up to meet the ridge above.
name: Dark Misty Glade

desc:
Twisted vines smother the gnarled oaks in this pitch black wood. The oaks tower
upwards into the canopy, completely choked by growths of vines which tighten
around their trunks. A thick, yellow substance drips from the oaks, releasing a
strange smell and fine mist that hovers above the forest floor, which is covered
with oddly-warped plants that seem to grasp towards any movement.
name: Dark Oak Grove

desc:
The pair of ancient alders grow intricately upwards, their boles and branches
scoured by time. Vastly larger than the surrounding trees, they reach up
together and touch, forming a dark canopy through which only a small amount of
light breaks through. A deep layer of brown leaves covers the ground, where
grasses and ferns grow. A fine mist flows in from the west.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
The forest stands tall, with stout oaks that rise with knotted branches up to
the thick canopy above. Hung from their twisted arms, snake a large number of
wicked vines that bite deep into the oaks, leeching sap from their dark boles.
The falling ridge from the northwest halts its course, revealing a tiny, dark
hole in the limestone wall beneath a tangle of roots covered with wet moss.
name: Dark Oak Grove

desc:
Boles of oak and alder rise up to a dark canopy completely choked by strong
vines. A thick liquid drips from the trees as the twisted vines leech the sap
from the tree trunks, leaving a sour smell hanging in the air. The forest floor
is covered with small grasses, a fine layer of moss, and leaves that sit among
the gnarled roots that crawl into the limestone bedrock and soil.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
The wet, mossy slope descends into a dark, limestone sinkhole, lined by several
twisted oaks. A small foul-smelling pond of standing water rests among the green
reeds and warped boles. A large, limestone slab juts out into the pond beyond
the black mud, near some gently-floating lily pads and watercress.
name: Depression in a Dark Forest

desc:
Thick roots dangle from the limestone ceiling above as they continue downwards
into the floor of this dark cave. The floor is covered with the countless,
gnawed bones of creatures in varying states of decay. As the cave progresses
towards the back of the hollow, it rises upwards into a tangled mess of roots,
where a dark hole can be spotted leading further up into the darkness.
name: Entangled Cavern

desc:
The grassy, leaf-covered bedrock dips into a wide sinkhole. The entrance of a
root-covered cavern gapes to the west, the result of the underlying cavern
buckling and sending the overlying slabs of limestone crashing downwards. Above
the rubble, an entwining forest canopy looms, lifted by huge oaken boles from
the dark wood that line the rigid, unclimbable walls of the sinkhole.
name: Dark Sinkhole

desc:
A long escarpment of weathered limestone rises to the south as it extends from
the west towards the east, where it bends northwards. At the base of the scarp,
a number of shrubs grow among the heaps of rubble and grass that stretch across
the land. The ground dips into a sinkhole towards the west, where a dark forest
can be seen towering far above the land on a raised crag that heads northwards.
name: Before an Escarpment

desc:
Rocky crags poke out of the raised ridge in the west, which is topped by a dark
forest with an entangled canopy full of wicked vines. The moors are covered with
green bracken as they extend between a falling ridge towards the east and a
rising escarpment in the south, which shows a colourful range of limestone
layers.
name: Moorland

desc:
Grasses and pebbles cover the craggy slope to the west, under the canopy of the
dark forest that grows on the raised ridge bank in the west. Yellow and green
heather covers the ground of the moors, that stretch onward to the east among
the many woody shrubs and limestone slabs that cover the landscape.
name: Moorland

desc:
The field is covered in soft ferns, grasses, and a delicate layer of moss. It
sits under the canopy of a dark forest that grows on the top of the craggy ridge
in the west. The limestone bedrock is exposed in several locations among dirty
roots as the earth climbs up a steep, overgrown ridge to the north.
name: Bracken Field

desc:
An ancient hall has been carved out of the limestone cavern that leads to a
barricade to the east. Large rocks and sparse grasses litter the mouth of this
timeworn entrance, leaving the stone stairs riddled with cracks and debris. The
crude, wooden barricade in the east smells strongly of fir, as a dank draft
rushes past its axe-hewn wooden spikes to the white ruins above.
name: Decrepit Hall

desc:
A calm valley of green heather grows in the protective hold of a ridge as the
land bends around the valley to the east and north. Small ferns and stones cover
the ground among the grasses that make up the thickets that grow around here.
The steppes expand outwards to the south, in a range of verdant colour. Tiny,
woody shrubs dot the landscape as it slowly extends across the horizon.
name: Sheltered Valley

desc:
Several patches of clover grow in this field, which stretches across the wide
steppe in between several ridges. Heather, ferns, and woody shrubs grow across
the landscape regardless of where the limestone bedrock exposes itself. A
shrouded, dark forest can be seen on a raised ridge to the west, and an even
larger and darker wood can be seen in the distant east, shrouded in shadow.
name: Field of Clover

desc:
The foot of a ridge ends here as it drops harshly from its unclimbable heights
towards a bracken field. Several large boulders sit in disarray at the bottom of
the slope, among the lichen that smother it. Verdant shrubs, ferns, and grasses
grow on the ground that leads up to the shrub-covered northeastern ridge, upon
which a trail can be seen climbing up into the distance.
name: End of a Ridge

desc:
A weedy embankment of heather lines the grass- and pebble-strewn trail, as it
weaves its way across the flat steppes towards a ridge in the north. Several
bushes grow some distance away from the trail, but all sights are dwarfed by an
enormous, black forest in the far east that towers over the surroundings.
name: Grassy Trail

desc:
The trail courses along the grassy steppe, lined with woody shrubs and yellow
and green grasses, until it reaches the foot of a grey, limestone ridge, where
it rises up on a switchback. Spreading around the trail is a wide steppe,
carpeted in heather and rugged patches of shrubs among the boulders and stones.
name: Grassy Trail

desc:
The vegetation becomes increasingly sparse as it moves deeper into the shadows
of the towering crags that line the ridge. Only patches of grass grow among the
thorns and rubble that litter the ground of a rough, descending slope from the
walls. Several large, knife-like boulders sit in the middle of valley, where
they fell from above and then pierced the dark valley floor.
name: Rocky Coombe

desc:
The grassy trail bends towards the north and west, as it reaches an eroding,
limestone escarpment to the south. The weathered cliffs are highly unstable, as
shown by an unsteady pile of debris that has fallen next to the pebbled trail
embankment, crushing all the vegetation that grew below it. Further to the
north, the heather grows undisturbed and the land widens into a plain.
name: Bend in a Grassy Trail

desc:
The trail bends through a deep crevice as it heads towards the east and south,
through the many exposed layers of limestone that make up a precarious, towering
escarpment. Among the grey rubble and jagged stones that litter the ground, is a
sparse carpet of yellow and green grasses that grow under the cliffs on either
side of the steep, unclimbable banks.
name: Trail through an Escarpment

desc:
Before the trail enters the valley stretching far to the south, it is split by a
tall fault that reaches many miles into the plateau to the west. The trail shows
signs of being travelled, although not very often, and it is wide enough to let
a rider travel comfortably. On the other hand, it offers little cover for
someone who wishes to pass by unseen.
name: Trail in a Wide Valley

desc:
The trail is part of the route for travelling between the Misty Mountains far
away to the northwest and through the Mirkwood to the southeast. It seems little
used although the earth is packed hard by heavy boots occasionally trampling it;
the trail gives a slightly rough impression and a few bones, some gnawed upon,
can be found here and there along the way.
name: Trail in a Wide Valley

desc:
Instead of just following the trail southwards or eastwards, one has the
possibility of going west from here, climbing the up side of the valley, to
later perhaps follow the valley on its western side. Going north is made
impossible by the nature of a ridge that was created by the fault.
name: Bend on a Valley Trail

desc:
This part of the valley is particularly narrow, with less than thirty yards
between the very steep sides. The walls consist of hard packed earth without
handholds, making any climbing attempts impossible. The sides of the walls are
covered with holes about the size of the rocks that seem to cling to the cliff
face high above. Some scattered bones rest on the ground among boulders.
name: Trail through a Narrow Valley

desc:
Leaving the trail to the east, a quickly narrowing fault leads far into the west
between walls of earth. The earth on the sides is too loose to let anyone climb
them successfully. The undergrowth here is not very spread out and could have
been trampled down by hunters passing by, wanting easier access to the prey
hunted down into the fault.
name: Beginning of a Fault

desc:
Reaching further into the western craggy landscape of the fault, this part of
the land is too rough to give any idea of what might lie ahead or behind. Far
above, on the northern side, a forest grows, making a significant contrast to
the grassy ground of the fault. One wishing to visit the forest, would however,
have to find a way around, because of the huge height difference.
name: Jagged Fault

desc:
The fault digs westward into the surrounding plateaus, making an east-west
passage. Little grass grows on the ground, where as the walls to the north and
south are of hard packed earth, without any handholds. Their vertical angle
forces the traveller to choose another route.
name: Jagged Fault

desc:
The natural fault in the ground comes to an abrupt end as it widens and hits a
cliff wall to the west. Some stones of different sizes have tumbled down the
slide and lie in untidy piles. Some remnants of the animals are slowly decaying
into dust at the cliff wall. Far above, some branches stretching south from the
forest to the north can be sighted. The steep walls are impossible to climb and
the only possible route follows the fault eastwards.
name: Fault beneath a Canopy

desc:
The damp soil makes this a perfect growing area for many kinds of mosses. All
stones, big and small, are completely covered with lichen on their northern
sides. To the west lies a north-south going trail, while the plains continue
towards the south. Movement northwards is made impossible by a limestone cliff.
name: Mossy Field

desc:
The field extends southwards to a higher plateau with a steep side covered with
the same short grass that grows on the ground around here. The soil here,
however, becomes damper as it approaches the vivid field in the north. To the
west a trail can be seen going down into a valley in the far distance.
name: Field next to a Plateau

desc:
A deep chasm entrance, nearly covered by several huge boulders, splits the
northwest ridge that lines the valley as it bends towards the east. The valley
pass is surrounded by two crags that have eroded, revealing their sedimentary
layers to the open air. On the ground of the pass grows a deep thicket of grass
and shrubs among the limestone rocks that increase in density near the cliffs.
name: Rocky Valley Pass

desc:
The dark, twisting chasm widens into a shallow hole littered with dry branches
and shattered bones. The stench of rotting meat clings to the air as it becomes
increasingly apparent that some animal has made this space its den. Rubble and
layers of crumbling stone litter the ground that make the chasm difficult to
navigate as it continues to the west among many fallen boulders.
name: Within a Deep Chasm

desc:
Shadows drape from the dark, overhanging precipices into a deep chasm that
twists and turns its way through the entombing rock on either side. A tangled
heap of rotten rope lies to the northwest within the rubble of a landslide that
closed off the passage. The sky barely breaks through the jagged crags up above
where another rope can be seen stretching across the chasm to the other side.
name: Bottom of a Deep Chasm

desc:
An old rope spans the divide of a great chasm, allowing passage to a strangely
shaped rock formation that towers in the north. The taut rope sways gently as
weight is placed upon its long length that dips into the dark chasm below. The
twisting chasm seems to curve around the rock formation from the southeast,
making the weathered, yet sturdy, rope the only possible way there and back.
name: Upon an Old Rope

desc:
A strangely-shaped tower of hardened sandstone climbs upwards into three steep
peaks. The tower of rock is surrounded by a deep chasm and the only way off of
it is to the south. A boulder in the south has been coiled with loops of rope
whose ends then extend across the chasm. Hidden in between the sandstone spires
grows a small sanctuary of grasses, blossoms, and bushes in the shade.
name: Oddly-shaped Rock Formation

desc:
Balancing on the verge of a plateau, this field offers a great view of the lands
below. The northern slope is too steep to take a mount down, and the climb does
indead seem safe only for a skilled climber. The ground is covered with short
grass of a darker kind that continues into the plains to the south.
name: Brink of a Plateau

desc:
High above the valley, this field has had little protection from the erosion of
wind and rain. In the north-western corner, little but loose earth is left,
while further from the edge of the plateau some grass has managed to grow a few
inches above the ground. The weeds dig their roots into the sparse soil,
clinging dearly to whatever nutrients still remain in the wind-blown area.
name: Brink of a Plateau

desc:
The most obvious thing of this flat plateau, is the difference in height
compared to the thick forest less than a mile to the northwest. Between the two
different areas, however, lies a deep fault with sides so vertical that climbing
down it simply is not an option. A path leads across the valley to the south
opposite a distance trail to the east that might lead past this fault.
name: Atop a North-South Valley

desc:
Most notable on the grassy field, are the signs of big boulders that rest in the
west on a spot with no grass. The soil there seems to be packed very hard, as if
the soil was of poorer quality. From the edge of the ridge, a clear view over
the trail is accessible.
name: Field near a Ridge

desc:
There are some smaller fields following the stretching valley to the east. The
plains to the west, however, cannot be reached because of some very thick brush.
Directly to below, another field can be seen lying next to the trail. A better
view of that area can probably be seen from the next field over.
name: Above a Valley

desc:
A large, wooden barricade, which reaches the height of a man, has been
constructed on the western side of the cavern. Below it, in a thick layer of
dark mud, rocks and garbage litter a spur of water leading to the eastern hall.
The worn look of the western hall has given way to smooth walls and a high
ceiling. The exception is the southern wall, which has been reinforced.
name: Barricaded Hall

desc:
The dark mud from the west has thinned slightly as the water spurs lead into a
small, rune-covered culvert in the eastern wall. Several crumbling pillars rise
up, touching the ceiling, as they support the hall from collapsing. The aged
limestone has held up very well to time, but even such excellent craftsmanship
has its limits, and cracks are beginning to form from the stress of the ages.
The original wall to the south has crumbled, but it has been rebuilt more
recently.
name: Crumbling Hall

desc:
This section of the ancient, limestone hall has held up exceptionally well
through time, except for the partly crumbled wall to the north. Faint remains of
elven runes line the pillars and walls with intricate detail, but have been
viciously scrawled out. A large statue stands to the west, its missing head has
been replaced by a giant, rusty, iron eye.
name: Crumbling Hall

desc:
The enormous pillars in this room hint at the former grandeur of the main hall
before everything was plundered and fell into decay. Shadows drape from the
ragged scars where banners hung and ornate awnings laid. A dim light shines
among the filth and rubble from behind a large, matted curtain to the south.
name: Pitted Hall

desc:
Filth and matted straw covers the ground of this dimly lit room in an effort to
contain the foul, pasty-white substance that clings to everything. Small chirps
echo across the giant hall, while the walls slowly rise up to a smaller natural
cavern above. A slight breeze rushes down along the moist fir and oak platform
that rises up to the small mossy cavern to the upper east.
name: Ruined Hall

desc:
Several huge murals tell the tale of this broken armoury, which has been
hideously devastated. Only a couple of frames remain; these show the forging of
a crystal vial that pierces the darkness. Several coffers lie here which have
been cracked open and emptied, leaving only shadows among the cobwebs and rust.
name: Demolished Armoury

desc:
The hewn walls of limestone bedrock bear the rough scars of pickaxe blows, which
increase in prominence towards the southern end of the room. A matted drapery
encloses the smoke-filled area where a table etched with runes has been placed
in the centre. The pungent scent of herbs and incense fills the air. A lit
candle creates an eerie mood to the room.
name: Smoky, Draped Room

desc:
Planks surround the walls of this dark cave. A crude, wooden staircase made of
fir and rope rises up to a hatch that is connected to the eastern wall. Nailed
into the muddy, wooden floor in the middle of the room is a dark, iron cover,
from which rushing water can be heard. The western wall is blocked by a sturdy
door, while the northern passage leads onward into the darkness.
name: Boarded up Cave

desc:
An immense cave rises up into the pitch-black darkness above, where menacing
stalactites hang from the ceiling. The western cave wall arches up to a steep,
unclimbable ledge where a rusty iron grate has been fitted into the stone. The
floor is covered with pickaxe-hewn rubble that seems to have been expelled from
the grate above. The eastern floor of the cavern is covered with stalagmites.
name: Rising Cave

desc:
This cavern is dug in the limestone with rough pickaxe blows. The floor of the
cavern is covered by a thin layer of jet-black feathers. The walls have many
markings like someone or something had scratched it with claws, and there are
also claw markings on the floor. The only exit is via the raised metal grill to
the north.
name: Horrid Cave

desc:
A pickaxe-hewn tunnel stretches down towards the west, away from the sturdy,
timber door that has been built into the wall to the east. Small piles of rocks
lie strewn about, along with some extra timber boards from the construction of
the door. The tunnel sides are hewn, and are about the width of a large troll.
name: Hewn Tunnel

desc:
The dry tunnel curves from the east to the south into a natural depression in
the tunnel wall. Small squeaks can be heard, and the guano deposits get heavier
as the tunnel leads southwards. The walls of the tunnel are marked with scars
from where the pickaxes struck, and rubble lies on the floor, along with some
straw. A subtle draft carries the foul smell of decay from the south.
name: Hewn Tunnel

desc:
The ground lowers into a still-growing and decades-old pile of bat guano. The
curved cavern walls are streaked with white lines that go all the way down to
the floor. The cavern rises to an unclimbable height up above, where more bat
squeaks can be heard. Some bones of unfortunate critters litter the ground.
name: Filthy Depression

desc:
The limestone tunnel opens up into a larger cavern cut off by a rusted, iron
grate. Pickaxe blows mar the tunnel walls as they slowly lead downwards into a
dark hatch made of timber. The western wall is heavily hewn, but oddly smooth,
as a small, rectangular outline runs across its surface. A small, disarrayed
pile of limestone rubble and decaying wooden struts, sits in the northwest.
name: Cavern Ledge

desc:
A thick mat of orange needles blankets the ground among the fern fronds that
grow amid the fir boles. Wrinkled roots curl among the limestone rocks as each
tree thrusts itself upright into the impenetrable canopy. The prickly branches
are densely woven together, making travel through the dark wood difficult.
name: Dark Fir Grove

desc:
An orange dome opens up above, which has a painted crown of stalactites that
drip over a massive stalagmite in the centre of the cave. The wicked-looking
rock formations range in colour from red to black, and leave watery streaks of
white leading down to the cave below. The pillar below looks like a yellow pile
of hardened baubles, around which a pool of water has collected.
name: Dripping Grotto

desc:
A natural drapery of rock flows over the ceiling of this cave, as a gentle
stream of water trickles along its edge towards the southern grotto. The many
slender blades of the drapery course along with one another in a harmonious span
of colour, ranging from spotted yellows to dark reds. The cave is rather cramped
compared to the other caves, due to the size of the formations above.
name: Small Cavern

desc:
The entire cave is bathed in a gentle, green light that emanates from the soft
moss, which covers the limestone walls. A sandy shore in the south, peppered
with moss-covered rocks, extends down to a silent and calm lake. The moss dims
on the rock wall in several places, revealing depressions that create a natural
stair that climbs up to the dripping cavern above.
name: Wet Cavern

desc:
A deep, underwater crack spews extremely cold water forth from some unknown
source. The water rushes noisily southwards through the dark, rocky stream
channel opposite a slight beveled, ledge in the north, with some natural
handholds that lead upwards. The northern cavern above has been boarded off with
some dark timber and a dark, heavily rusted, iron cover.
name: Underground Stream

desc:
This dark cave bares the marks of pickaxe blows from when it was enlarged to
hold the many crates and chests that reside within it. Placed deep within the
caves, the air chills and protects their contents from the outside world. At the
far end of the cavern, within an alcove, sits a lone desk made of dark oak. The
smell of herbs, paper, and dust hangs heavy in the stale air.
name: Hidden Cavern

desc:
The dusty cave is covered with brown dirt and rubble that lies in several huge
and disorganized piles. There is so much rubble in the way that the location of
a possible tunnel leading elsewhere is nearly hidden. A slight draft that
carries moisture can be barely detected leading towards the south.
name: Rubble-strewn Cavern

desc:
These cramped tunnels twist and turn into several subpassages. The bottom of the
tunnels are covered with a fine layer of soft, dark mud. Several small pools of
water can be found at various locations in the dips and bends. A large crack,
leading down a muddy passage, can be found in the middle of the biggest passage.
The sound of water can be heard running below the muddy crack.
name: Wet Tunnels

desc:
The blackened cavern walls have been worn smooth by the constant passage of
water over the millennia. Cold water races through the underground stream,
gurgling noisily as it bends around the passages. The bottom of the stream can
not be reached easily, but a muddy bottom can be barely seen through the cold,
clear depths of the rushing water.
name: Underground Stream

desc:
The underground stream suddenly takes a turn to the north where a huge pile of
rotting logs has accumulated as the passage narrows. A putrid layer of slimey,
yellow foam and debris sits on top of the water like a blanket, unable to drift
away because of the blockage. The dark cave is smooth due to all the fine mud
that is caked with decaying vegetation. The noise of water can be heard from
beyond the slimey logs.
name: Underground Stream

desc:
A tiny, cramped chamber sits here where two muddy tunnels meet. The noise of
running water can be heard roaring from the northern tunnel, making hearing
anything else nearly impossible. The fine, soft dirt that covers the floor of
this cavern sinks underfoot. It looks like the mud at the bottom of either
tunnel is wet and will stick to anyone who tries to climb up or down them.
name: Noisy, Cramped Cavern

desc:
A formation of yellow and orange limestone bubbles flows over the ledge that
overlooks overhead before sliding down several yards to a tunnel that leads
northwards. A sound of flowing water can be heard from above. Stalagmites and
stalagtites surround the cave among the puddles that shimmer with each dripping
droplet of water.
name: Under the Cavern Ledge

desc:
Huge, gnarled roots have cracked the limestone ceiling, allowing water to run
down their lengths, and staining the ceiling with various colours of black, red,
and orange. Each root looks like a curtain made of cloth, as it slowly forks and
splits getting smaller and finer. The roots sway back and forth in the drafts
that waft through these caves. The noise of running water can be heard from the
north and south.
name: Root-covered Ledge

desc:
Water is flowing slowly here it seems as though there are underground routes
where water keeps on running. Most of the routes are very narrow, and they are
not possible to enter, and the pull isn't strong enough to give any problems for
a swimmer. The underground pool continues to the west of here. A small ledge can
be barely spotted towards the east, a little higher on the wall.
name: Underground Pool

desc:
The cold, clear water here flows quickly to the north, and the current has a
strong pull to the north, which may give problems for a mediocre swimmer. The
tunnel is narrow here, and the walls of the tunnel have been smoothed by the
flow of water. The tunnel seems to widen to the north, and the noise of the
steady flow of water fills the tunnels.
name: Water-filled Tunnel

desc:
The walls of the cavern are slick and black here, and there is so much water
that it is not possible to walk here. The water is very cold, and the bottom of
the pool is barely visible. A small rocky formation protrudes out of the pool in
the middle area. It looks like there are bones and remnants of dead animals
surrounding the rock.
name: Far Edge of an Underground Pool

desc:
This narrow, cramped tunnel looks of entirely natural origin. The rock walls are
uneven and the tunnel shift in size from a narrow crawlspace to being high and
wide enough to drive a wagon through. Common for the entire tunnel is that it's
wet and slimy.
name: Uneven Tunnel

desc:
Rough cavern walls of natural bedrock surrounds a murky pool that has formed in
a depression here. A small cavern opening to the east is the only way to access
this cave. There are some stalactites in the ceiling above where water slowly
seeps in to keep the pool full.
name: Stale, Murky Pool

desc:
Colourful wildflowers cover the slopes among the green grasses that fill the
valley; the enclosing ridges widen slightly in the east. The black soil is rich
and sinks slightly as it gives away underfoot. A couple of young birch trees
grow on the western slope, near the entrance to a narrow valley pass.
name: Narrow Valley

desc:
The grassland dips in between the feet of two ridges, where it widens into a
broad plain of green heather. The grass runs southwards across the valley, where
a dark marsh can be seen shrouded in faint mist. A large animal has been grazing
on the steppe here, leaving deep hoof impressions in the wet soil and sheered
grass. A patch of bare bedrock rises out of the valley floor to the east.
name: Sloping Grassland

desc:
The ridge crests at the top of a grassy hill, where the bedrock disappears under
the thick heather and soil. A view of an enormous, black forest in the far east
fills the horizon, as a dark marsh in the southwest lurks in a cloak of grey
mist. The vivid grasslands of heather stretch onward through the wetlands and
stones to some dark crags on the southern ridge.
name: Grassy Ridge

desc:
The wet grasslands sink underfoot as water saturates the sod from some unknown
source. The flat area is covered in a vivid, multicoloured array of grasses that
ranges from near violet to dark green. Several bunches of strong smelling weeds
grow here, displaying white blooms among the wild flowers.
name: Grassy Wetland

desc:
The grasslands extend out towards the north and west, right before a rocky ridge
that climbs to the south. The earth is wet and sinks underfoot when any weight
is placed upon it. A variety of plants grows in the soil, displaying a large
number of shapes among the thickets of long grasses. A couple round droppings,
shaped like pies and filled with grass, litter the ground.
name: Wet Lowland

desc:
The rising ridge reaches an overhanging precipice, where a large pile of rubble
have accumulated at its face. Dense thickets of grasses and shrubs cover the
slope of the ridge. Further to the east, a distant, dark forest can be seen,
while to the north, an expanse of wet lands and marshes shrouded in mist covers
the wide valley, before disappearing into another pair of ridges.
name: Rising Ridge

desc:
The ridge rises up to a dark limestone precipice in the south and west, where
two separate ridges can be seen: one falling towards the north and the other
bending towards the east. Several green shrubs cover the ground, forming vivid
thickets amidst the grasses that grow in the rich soil. A dark marsh can be seen
in the mist to the northwest, while wetlands extend further to the east.
name: Rising Ridge

desc:
A marsh extends to the north, through a deep mire filled with black muck. Lush
grasses grows in small clumps around the wet meadow, where the land dips around
the small pools of water. A muddy wallowing-pool of filthy water, encircled by
crushed grass and strewn grime, lies here with hoof tracks leading to and from
it. The land rises as the southern, grass-covered ridge falls into the marsh.
name: Wet Meadow

desc:
Sedges and reeds grow in a small row to the north, as the land opens up into a
foul-smelling bog. Several pools of mud run through the land, split by large
islands of multicoloured grass and weeds. The stench of decaying plant matter
fills the air as a fine, grey mist floats gently on the surface of the water.
name: Muddy Swamp

desc:
A stinking bog of grime spreads out across the land, filling it with layers of
mulch and pools of muddy water. Lush grasses grow in huge mounds where the land
rises up above the muck; there is some evidence of a large animal grazing. The
area is filled with a fine mist that reeks with the stench of decaying plant
matter. The foul marsh continues towards the southwest, shrouded in grey mist.
name: Muddy Fen

desc:
A misty marshland, surrounded by rows of dark reeds and sedges, courses around
the murky channels that snake through the bog. A fine, wispy layer of grey mist
moves above the muck, where thick patches of long grasses rise above the foul,
bracken water. A deep and black pond stirs to the northwest, where it sits under
the face of a rocky precipice, enclosed by the surrounding ridge-walls.
name: Among the Misty Sedges

desc:
The intricacy of this entanglement of brush creates an almost mystifying network
of snarled passages, which dart to and fro in the vegetation. The paths have
probably been created by wildlife inhabitants of this greenery, although none
can be seen and no other sounds than a low rustling of grass can be heard.
Reaching far above the tall brush blocks the view to the surrounding areas.
name: Entangled Bushes

desc:
The intricacy of this entanglement of brush creates an almost mystifying network
of snarled passages, which dart to and fro in the vegetation. The paths have
probably been created by wildlife inhabitants of this greenery, although none
can be seen and no other sounds than a low rustling of grass can be heard.
Reaching far above the tall brush blocks the view to the surrounding areas.
name: Entangled Bushes

desc:

of snarled passages, which dart to and fro in the vegetation. The paths have
probably been created by wildlife inhabitants of this greenery, although none
can be seen and no other sounds than a low rustling of grass can be heard.
Reaching far above the tall brush blocks the view to the surrounding areas.
name: Entangled Bushes

name: Entangled Bushes
